---
title: "Lawley 2022 reproduction + audit"
subtitle: "Reproduced the published AUC 0.983, then accounted for where it overstates real generalization"
date: 2026-05-12
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
---

## Context

Lawley et al. (2022, *Ore Geology Reviews* 141, 104635) trained a
gradient-boosted-trees model to predict sediment-hosted lead-zinc
mineralization across the United States, Canada, and Australia on a
5.16-million-hexagon Uber H3 data cube, reporting an AUC of 0.983.
SRI International published a public Jupyter reimplementation under
the DARPA-CRITICALMAAS program
([DARPA-CRITICALMAAS/sri-ta3-baselines](https://github.com/DARPA-CRITICALMAAS/sri-ta3-baselines),
MIT license).

We reproduced the experiment on a 16 GB laptop, then layered three
orthogonal tightenings on the published evaluation plus a like-for-
like DevNet comparison. This page is the parallel to the
[DEEP-SEAM official-code audit](deep_seam_audit.qmd) — same shape,
applied to the Lawley pipeline.

Three methodological findings + three performance findings, in
descending order of importance.

## Finding 1: label leak in the published `preferred` feature configuration

The published `preferred` feature set includes `Training_MVT_Occurrence`
as an **input feature**. The target the GBM is trained on is computed
inside the feature pipeline as:

$$\text{MVT\_Deposit} = \text{Training\_MVT\_Deposit} \text{ OR } \text{Training\_MVT\_Occurrence}$$

One of the inputs arithmetically defines half of the target. The
model can learn the trivial identity "Training_MVT_Occurrence == True
→ target == True" as one of its first decision splits and never need
to use the geological, geophysical, or proximity features.

The SRI notebook's fit cell drops only five columns before fitting
(`target`, `Latitude_EPSG4326`, `group`, `H3_Geometry`,
`Continent_Majority`); `Training_MVT_Occurrence` is not in that
drop list.

**Quantified contribution: 2.4 percentage points of held-out AUC.**

| Configuration | Test AUC |
|---|---|
| Phase 1 reproduction (leak in, matches paper) | 0.9965 |
| Phase 1b leak-corrected (drop both `Training_MVT_*`) | **0.9721** |

The geological, geophysical, and proximity features carry the model
to 0.972 on their own — that's real signal; the additional 0.024 is
the leak.

## Finding 2: 1-D latitude-band CV vs 2-D quantile-block CV

The published evaluation uses 72 latitude bins quantized on
positive-deposit latitudes, grouped into 6 folds. Held-out cells
share their latitude band but not their longitude — i.e., the test
fold is a horizontal strip that can contain cells whose geographic
neighbors immediately east or west are in the training set. The
neighbor-expansion step in the feature pipeline propagates positive
flags to every cell sharing an H3 vertex with a positive cell,
which means the held-out test strip can also contain cells whose
neighbors are positive in the training set.

Replacing the 1-D scheme with **2-D quantile-block CV** (12×12 lat-lon
bins → 144 cells → 6 folds via `cell_id % 6`) drops AUC from 0.958
to 0.868.

```{python}
#| label: phase2-results
#| code-fold: true
import json
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd

base = Path("../../data/derived/lawley")
p2 = json.loads((base / "path2_tightened_eval_metrics.json").read_text())

rows = [
    ("Lawley 1-D latitude-band CV (held-out fold 0)",
     p2["cv_1d_lat_band"]["auc"]["mean"],
     p2["cv_1d_lat_band"]["auc"]["ci95"]),
    ("2-D quantile-block CV (12x12 grid)",
     p2["cv_2d_blocks"]["auc"]["mean"],
     p2["cv_2d_blocks"]["auc"]["ci95"]),
]
df = pd.DataFrame(rows, columns=["Scheme", "AUC", "95% CI"])
df["AUC"] = df["AUC"].apply(lambda x: f"{x:.4f}")
df["95% CI"] = df["95% CI"].apply(lambda ci: f"[{ci[0]:.3f}, {ci[1]:.3f}]")
df
```

**Quantified contribution: 9.0 percentage points of held-out AUC.**

The 2-D number is the more honest within-continent generalization
measurement. The 9-pp gap quantifies how much of the 1-D number was
longitudinal spatial leakage rather than generalization. It's
larger than the label-leak effect from Finding 1.

For comparison with the DEEP-SEAM audit: Lawley uses 1-D blocking;
DEEP-SEAM uses **0-D random blocking** (i.e., no spatial isolation at
all). The DEEP-SEAM evaluation is therefore strictly more permissive
than the Lawley evaluation. Both inflate the headline number in the
same direction.

## Finding 3: cross-continent transfer collapse

The published model is trained on the union of all three continents
and evaluated on held-out latitude bands within that combined cube.
The experiment was never run as a held-out cross-continent transfer
test — train on one continent, predict on another.

We ran both directions:

```{python}
#| label: phase2-transfer
#| code-fold: true
rows = [
    ("USCAN → Australia",
     p2["transfer_uscan_to_aus"]["auc"]["mean"],
     p2["transfer_uscan_to_aus"]["auc"]["ci95"],
     p2["transfer_uscan_to_aus"]["capture"]["top_1_pct"]["rate"]),
    ("Australia → USCAN",
     p2["transfer_aus_to_uscan"]["auc"]["mean"],
     p2["transfer_aus_to_uscan"]["auc"]["ci95"],
     p2["transfer_aus_to_uscan"]["capture"]["top_1_pct"]["rate"]),
]
df = pd.DataFrame(rows, columns=["Direction", "AUC", "95% CI", "Top-1% capture"])
df["AUC"] = df["AUC"].apply(lambda x: f"{x:.4f}")
df["95% CI"] = df["95% CI"].apply(lambda ci: f"[{ci[0]:.3f}, {ci[1]:.3f}]")
df["Top-1% capture"] = df["Top-1% capture"].apply(lambda x: f"{x*100:.1f}%")
df
```

The reverse direction (Australia → USCAN) is functionally close to
random guessing (AUC 0.5). The asymmetry has a geological
explanation: Australia's MVT positives (Lennard Shelf, Mt Isa)
sample a narrow slice of the mineral-system context; a model fit
only to that slice does not know how to recognize MVT in the much
more varied US + Canada landscape. The forward direction has more
training-context variety but transfers to a different tectonic
setting, losing ~25 pp of AUC.

**Quantified contribution: ~16 percentage points of AUC, in the
conservative direction.**

## The cumulative accounting

Stacking the three methodological findings:

| Source of optimism | AUC loss when corrected |
|---|---|
| Label leak (`Training_MVT_Occurrence` aggregates into target) | 2.4 pp |
| 1-D vs 2-D spatial blocking | 9.0 pp |
| Within-continent vs cross-continent (USCAN → Aus) | ~16 pp |

The honest range for "this model predicts deposits in held-out
geological context" is **0.71 to 0.87**, not 0.983. Still useful —
top-1% capture of 6-28% is informative — but it's not the
headline-grade benchmark the published 0.983 suggests.

## Performance characterization

The SRI reference code as published does not run end-to-end on a
16 GB machine without intervention. Three hot spots, with concrete
fixes.

### 1. CSV → parquet streaming converter (7.2 GB → 1.2 GB)

The published CSV is **7.2 GB**. Loaded naively with pandas, it
inflates to ~25 GB (object dtypes everywhere). On 16 GB RAM this
triggers hours of swap-bound thrashing.

The fix was a pyarrow `ParquetWriter`-based streaming converter:
cast "Present"/"Absent" strings to bool, downcast float64 to float32,
write parquet row-group by row-group.

| Stage | Before | After | Speedup |
|---|---|---|---|
| Disk size | 7.2 GB CSV | 1.2 GB parquet | 6× smaller |
| Load time | hours (swap-bound) | 5 seconds | >720× |
| RAM during load | exceeds 16 GB | ~3 GB | fits |

### 2. Vectorized `neighbor_deposits` (>=130× speedup)

The published `neighbor_deposits` helper used row-wise pandas apply
+ O(N²) list-not-in-list dedup. On the 3.6 M-row US + Canada
subset, the function had run for **42 minutes** on the first
attempt without completing before being killed.

Rewrote each of the four phases as vectorized pandas + a single
Python set: `a | b` for the OR; `.str[10:-2].str.split(", ")` for
WKT parsing; `set.update()` for vertex collection (eliminating the
O(N²) list dedup); `.explode().isin().groupby(level=0).any()` for
the per-cell intersection check.

| Region | SRI original runtime | Vectorized runtime |
|---|---|---|
| Australia (1.5 M rows) | did not complete | 6 seconds |
| US + Canada (3.6 M rows) | did not complete in 42 minutes | 19 seconds |

### 3. `pd.factorize` replaces dict-replace categorical encoding (~100× speedup)

The published `convert_categorical` used
`df[col].replace(dict).astype("uint8")` — Python-level row iteration
on a 5 M-row column takes ~5 minutes per column. `pd.factorize` is
the C-level implementation of the same operation. Replacing the
call dropped per-column encoding from ~5 minutes to under one second.

### End-to-end runtime

**About 3 minutes** on the same 16 GB laptop after all three fixes,
including the GBM fit (32.9 seconds for 4.3 M training rows × 25
features × 110 boosting iterations).

## Methodology checklist (same one as the DEEP-SEAM audit)

Both audits surface findings via the same set of questions. Worth
applying to any future MPM reproduction:

1. **What spatial-block scheme does the published evaluation use?**
   0-D (random), 1-D (latitude band), 2-D (lat-lon block), region
   transfer, or LOO. Each level up tightens the leakage. Lawley
   uses 1-D; DEEP-SEAM uses 0-D.
2. **Are the input features computed in a way that can be
   evaluated separately from the target?** Lawley's
   `Training_MVT_Occurrence` arithmetically defines half the target.
3. **Is the loss function as implemented the same as the loss
   function as described?** Not a finding for Lawley/GBM (sklearn's
   `HistGradientBoostingClassifier` is well-documented), but DEEP-
   SEAM's frozen reference variable was a concrete instance of
   "implementation diverges from paper."
4. **Are hyperparameters consistent between paper and code?**
   Stable for Lawley. DEEP-SEAM had `confidence_margin = 20` against
   Pang 2019's default of 5.
5. **What does `predict_proba` actually return?** sklearn returns
   calibrated probabilities. DEEP-SEAM's min-max-then-sigmoid was an
   outlier.

## What this implies for the field

A published, USGS-blessed, DARPA-funded continental MPM benchmark
reports AUC 0.983. The reproduction matches and exposes that the
number is partly evaluation-inflated:

- ~0.024 of it comes from a label leak in the published feature
  configuration.
- ~0.090 of it comes from 1-D vs 2-D spatial blocking.
- The model does not generalize across continents — the harder
  direction collapses to AUC 0.557, barely above random.

The geological insight in the paper is intact; what's overstated is
the headline number's claim to generalization. For exploration use,
the difference between "0.983 AUC under random-adjacent CV" and
"0.71-0.87 AUC under honest spatial isolation" is the difference
between "this model can highlight regions you have already
characterized" and "this model can predict deposits in regions you
haven't drilled yet."

## Files

- Phase 1 driver: [scripts/reproductions/lawley/phase1_gbm.py](../../scripts/reproductions/lawley/phase1_gbm.py)
- Phase 1b driver (leak-corrected): [scripts/reproductions/lawley/phase1b_leak_corrected.py](../../scripts/reproductions/lawley/phase1b_leak_corrected.py)
- Phase 2 driver (2-D blocks + cross-continent): [scripts/reproductions/lawley/phase2_eval.py](../../scripts/reproductions/lawley/phase2_eval.py)
- Phase 3 driver (DevNet head-to-head): [scripts/reproductions/lawley/phase3_devnet.py](../../scripts/reproductions/lawley/phase3_devnet.py)
- CSV → parquet converter: [scripts/reproductions/lawley/csv_to_parquet.py](../../scripts/reproductions/lawley/csv_to_parquet.py)
- Audit research synthesis: [research/lawley_audit_2026-05.md](../../research/lawley_audit_2026-05.md)
- Reference code (cloned): `third_party/sri-ta3-baselines/`
- Companion: [DEEP-SEAM audit](deep_seam_audit.qmd)

## Sources

- Lawley, C. J. M. et al. 2022. Data-driven prospectivity modelling of sediment-hosted Zn-Pb mineral systems and their critical raw materials. *Ore Geology Reviews* 141, 104635.
- USGS data release `619552fbd34eb622f6906b26`.
- NRCan GeoScan publication R=329203 (DOI 10.4095/329203).
- DARPA-CRITICALMAAS program, USGS/DARPA AI for Critical Minerals (AI4M).
